Inscription recording construction of a sphaeristrium by a father and son
Text fills the face, with traces of a larger line above; surviving line 1 is larger than the lines below.
Elegant tall and somewhat narrow v-cut letters with serifs. O is large, broad, and somewhat irregular in shape. R has curved and extended foot, P is open. G has straight foot.

The text maintains a justified left margin, but uneven right margin, with words regularly broken across the line-end, and no attempt to make the end of the text symmetrical or to match the content to the line-length / layout, as opposed to simply a consistent filling of the face until the text is complete.
Tall, relatively narrow letters, with short horizontal strokes often curving upwards (especially on T).

It is unclear exactly what the relationship of the two fragments to one another was.

Dedication to the deified Antoninus Pius
Erected after the death (161 CE) of Antoninus Pius
dedication to the deified antoninus pius

The inscription is set out as five lines of large monumental Latin letters centred on the upper portion of the epigraphic field on the front face below the moulding (the field is 60W x 85 H). Lines 1 and 4 are larger.
Letters have standard serifs and a wide regular module; a variety of hedera and interpuncts are employed.
Terminus post quem of 195 CE, the deification of Commodus, and likely belongs to the reign of Septimius Severus
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 08-07-2022
Text encoding: Tuuli Ahlholm, Jonathan Prag, edition source:
Six lines of large Latin letters which reduce in height, with a vacat below.
Fine monumental letters with occasional serif flourishes, and some taller letters. Lightly incised hederae for interpuncts.
Usually thought either to refer to C.Maesius Titianus, cos. 245 CE, or a likely relative of similar period.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Civico Baldassare Romano
Fazello, 1558, F. Thomæ Fazelli... de rebus Siculis decades duæ
Manganaro, 1982, I senatori di Sicilia e il problema del latifondo

Honorific for Titianus on the occasion of his assumption of the toga virilis

Eight lines of continuous Latin text. Text fills most of stone, with stone-carver attempting to centre text onto stone. Upright hedera in line 4, with right-facing tail. Two final lines smaller.  Occasional use of interpuncts, in lines 1, 2, 5 and 6: tick-shaped in line 1, and more Y-shaped in subsequent lines. Some sort of interpunct/squiggle in line 8. Representing a pointed tilde (~)
Letters are tall and elongated. Ornate serifs used on every letter. Letters extremely regular and spacing between letters is consistent.
3rd century CE on prosopographical grounds (but difficult to pin down more tightly) - so too Barbieri
honorific for titianus on the occasion of his assumption of the toga virilis
Text encoding: Tuuli Ahlholm, Alex Antoniou, edition source:
Honorific for Marcus Cestius, primus pilus and duumvir
Siciliae obiacentium insular et Bruttiorum antiquae tabulae cum animadversionibus Georgii Gualtheri
G. Gualtherus, Siciliae obiacentium insular et Bruttiorum antiquae tabulae cum animadversionibus Georgii Gualtheri (Panormi, 1624)
Gualtherus presents the text over four lines, the name filling line 1.
No details recorded, other than that Gualtherus marks a tall I in line 2 ('pilo').
Most likely to be one of the first generation of colonial settlers, and so probably Augustan in date, and (as Wilson 1990, p.369 n.58) unlikely to be later than Claudius.
honorific for marcus cestius, primus pilus and duumvir
Text encoding: Jonathan Prag, edition source: http://zotero.org/groups/382445/items/GIN6NANU
Three lines of Latin text (despite abrasions, it is likely that there are no missing lines), seemingly set in the middle of the face of the base. Use of one triangular interpunct (line 2).
Text is very deeply and widely incised. Text is regular, with serifs. Spacing between letters and lines is precise.
Likely an early colonist of Thermae Himeraeae, probably Augustan in date (after 21 BCE).
Korhonen,Soraci, 2019, Forme amministrative e scelte linguistiche nelle epigrafi e nelle monete della Sicilia romana
Honours for Procula, Plaetorius, Galeo and Longina

The text appears centered on the stone (uneven right margin, left margin missing). Line 1 is largest, with line 5 also given prominence by larger size than 2-4 and slightly greater spacing. The final three lines are smaller and less regular.
"Un periodo oscillante tra la seconda meta del I sec d.C. ed il II d.C" by letter forms)

honours for procula, plaetorius, galeo and longina
Honours for an unknown member of the equestrian order
Line 1 is considerably larger than the others, while the spacing of line 5 suggests a clear effort to keep the text centered. The final line consists of widely spaced abbreviations.

Elegantly v-cut letters, with serifs and interpuncts and reasonably wide modules. Occasional tall letters (Y line 4, 55mm, I line 6, 48 mm) and some exuberant strokes, such as the Y in line 4 or the Q in line 5.

The post of governor of Cyprus precedes the transfer of the province to the Senate in 22 BCE, but only provides a terminus post quem for the inscription, which in any case must surely belong after the foundation of the colonia in 21 BCE, but not, therefore, by much.
Romano, 1829, Antiche iscrizioni inedite appartenenti alla città di Termini Imerese
honours for an unknown member of the equestrian order
Funerary inscription for Gaius Popillius Priscus

Eight lines of Latin text, in centre (but not centred) of stone. Use of triangular interpuncts, although some are faint and indistinct. Interpuncts are not used between every word. Line 1 is notably larger, line 2 reduces, the other lines much smaller.

Tall, elongated letters. Serifs used on many of the letters. First line is chiselled deeper into stone than subsequent ones. After line three, letter forms begin to lean to the left and become irregular.

The dating is contested by Bivona (later C1 CE, post-69) and Manganaro (a date shortly after 310-313 CE), based upon the reading of the word after 'Victoria' in line 4 (Bivona prefers Ticiniana, Manganaro Licininia). The other features of the text would seem to favour a later first rather than a fourth-century date.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana, photo J.Prag 08.07.2022
funerary inscription for gaius popillius priscus
11 lines of regular neat wide letters of faily consistent size and spacing, with a regular left margin, almost entirely filling the available field.
regular letters iwth a faily wide square module, lacking visible interpuncts or serifs (but note the material); use of ligature at the end of line 2.
Bivona dates to 2nd century CE by lettering
Inscription recording construction of a portico by a duumvir
Three lines of large latin letters, centred, filling stone on all three lines. First line is larger.
Broad regular letters with limited serifs. Large triangular interpuncts between words, supraline and apex.
inscription recording construction of a portico by a duumvir
Rizzo, 1989, La menzione del lavoro nelle epigrafi della Sicilia antica: per una storia della mentalità
Neat and regular v-cut letters with simple terminal serifs.

Funerary inscription for Popillius of Pollentia
Two lines of Latin text. Use of triangular interpuncts between each word, although final visible interpunct on line 1 is shaped more like a tick or a sideways T
Letters are well formed, and relatively neat, with serifs (of particular note is the curled tail on P in line 1). Spacing between letters is regular.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2018-07-10
funerary inscription for popillius of pollentia

Traces are visible of three lines of text, but it is possible, from the spacing, that a fourth line (which would be line 3 of the text) is simply completely lost; the spacing would be tight, however, and more plausibly there is simply a vacat between lines 2 and 3, after the name and prior to the record of age at death, a simple formulation that finds multiple parallels at Termini.
Simply cut, somewhat irregular letters of varying width, with extended upper strokes and pronounced serifs.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2023-07-05.
Ephemeris epigraphica. Corporis inscriptionum Latinarum supplementum, edita iussu Instituti archaeologici Romani, 1872-1913
Three lines of Latin text. Lines 1-2 are centred on the stone, line 3 is at the extreme left of the stone. Use of large 'T' shaped interpuncts.

Letters are deeply incised. Letters in lines 1-2 are relatively straight, equally spaced and keep to the line, whereas line 3 is a little more varied in all of these features. Large serifs are used, particularly in the tail of the 'R' in line 1, and that of the 'L' in line 2.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2023-07-05
Text encoding: Jonathan Prag, Alex Antoniou, edition source:
Block of marmor Luculleum bearing quarry inscription

Two single lines of text, one on the left and one on the right side. Both texts run left to right, and so the text on the left runs from the rear of the stone to the front, while the text on the right runs from the front to the back. The text on the left is missing the opening letters, having been inscribed prior to the removal of the 'step' from the rear of the stone. The text on the right appears to be essentially intact.
Explicitly dated to the consulship of 108 CE
Lo Faso Pietrasanta, 1832-1842, Antichita Siciliane esplorate ed illustrate

Inscribed base of a dedication to Venus Victrix

A Latin text over 10 lines, engraved on the narrow front face
                                        of the base. The engraved face is 8 cm wide by 27.7 cm high.
                                        The majority of the words are run over onto the following
                                        line, with the final letters centred, suggesting that the
                                        visual effect was prioritised over the sense of the words.
                                        The text is large and well spaced in the upper part,
                                        becoming compressed towards the end, with the final lines
                                        overlapping.

Deeply, but rather irregularly cut letters.
                                    Y and T are tall, as is I in line 4; R is closed. Interpuncts in
                                    the form of a comma.
Korhonen suggests a date between the second half of the first and the end of the 2nd century CE

Letter of Iulius Paternus to the Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus

Latin text preserved over 12 lines. The first line is in slightly larger and more widely spaced letters. The text closes up over lines 2 and 3, 
                                    with a more or less regular size and spacing over the rest of the stone. Interpuncts are used sparingly in the text, and their placing appears 
                                    deliberately intended to emphasise certain elements. The letters are neatly cut, with limited serifs (but sometimes substantial feet to vertical strokes), 
                                    and a tendency towards the taller narrower letters common from the second/third centuries AD onwards; this is particularly noticeable with 
                                    the letter E, which is not always easy to distinguish when corroded. Tall 'I' is employed. P is generally open.
164—166 CE, on basis of restoration of first word as Armeniacis when Aurelius and Verus both held this title
letter of iulius paternus to the emperors marcus aurelius and lucius verus
List of magistrates of Catania from years 268—9 CE (prob., so manganaro, followed by K)

Inscribed statue base for Lucius Rubrius Proculus
Four lines of Latin text, centred on the stone and filling the face, slightly decreasing in size from line 1.

Large, plain, deeply cut letters. P is open, R and B are closed; some upright strokes (all letter I, some L and V) have a serif ascending upper right; plain interpuncts in lines 1 and 3; the numeral in line 3 is marked by a mid-bar.
Lettering and absence of filiation suggest a 2nd century CE date (Korhonen)

Funerary inscription for Aphrodito, a mime actress

Latin text over six lines, roughly centred on the plaque. Lines
                                    1-3 are more widely spaced and better centred; lines 4-6 are more compressed in
                                    the lower right.

Letters show some tendency to a cursive style, but the name in line 2
                                    is notably marked out in a more classical majuscule style. One instance of ligature
                                    in line 6. Use of simple hedera as interpuncts in lines 3-6.
2nd century CE or beginning of 3rd century CE

Remains of two lines of Latin letters across the stone, with traces of a third line in Greek below

Thinly incised letters with terminal points/serifs to the strokes. Greek letters are much smaller than Latin letters. The M tends lightly towards the cursive form, and some letters join at the foot; A in the Latin text has an upward sloping cross-bar; Greek text appears to have lunate epsilon.
1st century or beginning of 2nd century CE (Korhonen)

Bilingual sign advertising engraving of inscriptions

The text fills the face of the plaque, divided vertically by a deeply incised line into two unequal halves, with a column of Greek on the left (wider) and Latin on the right). Each column is approximately centred, and consists of seven lines of text, with the first two lines notably larger than the following five.
Greek text includes lunate sigma and epsilon; extended apex to delta and alpha. Latin text is is regular with serifs, open P, closed R. There are no word dividers or punctuation in either text.

Augustan or Julio-Claudian,
                                by the letter forms; also, archaic spelling of some of the Latin, with other Sicilian
                                parallels, rules out much later date (so Wilson 1990); Manni Piraino thinks later 2nd century CE

Dedication to the Concordia of the Agrigentines
Seven lines of large Latin letters, centred on the stone. The planning of the text is uneven, with awkward word-breaks (lines 1-2 and 3-4, and some compression at line end, especially line 5

Tall, slightly curved letters, mostly with minimal serifs. P is open; R is closed with long tail; E has short horizontals of equal length, slightly upward curving; M is four equal slanting strokes. In line 5 the O and C of 'procos' are intelinked, and the following O is small and mid-line. A large diagonal upward stroke is visible above the first letter of each of the civic ethnics; and a horizontal above the Q at the end of line 6. The letters all show traces of dark paint (modern?).

The letters of l. 3 appear narrower and more compressed than the others. The inscribed field is bordered by a moulded frame. In the lower part, decorative elements are visible, including a strip or handle, and a small vessel.
Elegant capital letters with slight serifs. P with open loop; A with vertical crossbar. Traces of interpuncts between words.

The first part of all six lines of the Greek text are preserved, but none are complete.

The letters are uniform and well cut, with pronounced serifs. A hollow triangular interpunct is used between all the onomastic elements (but apparently not elsewhere in the text; spaces are however observed between words, with or without interpunct). Theta has a detached middle bar; omicron is full size; epsilon has a detached short middle bar; mu reaches almost to the lower line in the middle, and the outer strokes are vertical.

Dedication of a statue to Ceres, by Iulius Acilius Hermes, sevir
The Latin text is engraved over two lines (letters c.20 mm high, but of uneven height), to a maximum length of 52 cm.

The letters show some irregularity, with fairly pronounced serifs, and lengthy tails to the letter R. The first two words are separated from the rest by a space; an interpunct is visible at the end of the dedicant’s name, and the final abbreviated phrase is separated from the rest by a large hedera (ivy leaf). The letter I in line 1 is frequently taller than the rest (but so too is the first L in line 1); the final I of line 1 is somewhat irregularly carved.
Statue can be dated stylistically to the late Antonine period (i.e. the 180s or 190s CE), and the text is compatible with such a date.
dedication of a statue to ceres, by iulius acilius hermes, sevir

The municipium of the Haluntini honours Gnaeus Pollienus
The text occupies five lines over the upper part of the front face.

Regular letters with a fairly square module, simply engraved without serifs. Alpha has broken bar; Kappa has full length arms; Mu has vertical first and last, with mid-strokes only descending half-way; omicron is full size; Pi has full length verticals, and slightly overlapping horizontal bar; omega is slightly open and nearly circular, with horizontal tails/feet.

The use of the term 'municipium' in Greek places this text almost certainly after the grant of Latin rights to Sicily by Julius Caesar (probably 46 BCE), but equally the use of Greek implies a date not later than the early Augustan period. The lettering is compatible with a late Hellenistic date.
Henzel, 2022, Honores inauditi: Ehrenstatuen in öffentlichen Räumen Siziliens vom Hellenismus bis in die Spätantike
the municipium of the haluntini honours gnaeus pollienus
Text encoding: Valentina Mignosa, Jonathan Prag, edition source:
Five lines of Greek letters, centred on the stone

Slightly crudely incised simple broad letters without serifs. Nu i sregular; epsilon has slightly shorter middle bar; mu has slightly inward leaning first and last strokes, the mid-strokes reach down to the base-line; rho has a small eye, not always fully closed; alpha has broken bar; omega varies between open circle closed by horizontal bar and closed ovoid form with horizontal bar; phi has a small circular eye; sigma has standard form with horizontal top and bottom storkes; omicron is approximately the same size as other letters.

Text encoding: Francesca Prado, edition source:
Fragment of a bilingual dedication to Priapus

Five lines of regularly spaced Greek letters followed by three lines of Latin letters. The first two lines of Latin text follow the same spacing as the Greek text; the final line is seemingly centered, more widely spaced (with interpuncts) and separated by a slightly greater interval from the lines above. Although the top of the stone is damaged, the visible space above line 1 is greater than that between the following lines.

Regular, well-cut Greek letters with clearly defined serifs at the end of strokes: Omicron, omega and theta all have rhomboid form; in some instances this is actually a pentagonal form, flat across the top; Omega is open at the bottom; Sigma is four bar and regular; Epsilon has equal horizontal strokes; Alpha has broken bar; the diagonal of the Nu starts and finishes well below/above the ends of the vertical strokes. The Latin letters show the beginnings of the move towards cursive forms: M has extended upper strokes and the midpoint reaches to the bottom of the line; A also has extended upper stroke and lacks a cross-bar; O is rounded, in contrast to the Greek text, as is Q with extended tail; P is open; R is closed; E has short upward slanting strokes; L has a long foot, forming almost a C.
Scan of photo in NSA 1922 p.494 fig.2

Vacat at top followed by parts of three lines of Latin letters, seemingly centred on the stone.
Two dedications to a Caesar by the Annii
Three lines of Latin letters centred on each of front and rear face, first line larger in both cases

The letters are neatly cut, with short serifs. P is open on both texts, R is closed. Text A has tall I in line three. The primary difference between Text A and Text B is in the technique, with the letters in A having a flat cut, whereas B is engraved with a more elegant 'v-cut' and more elegant interpuncts. It is likely that the two were carved by different stone-masons.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Regionale Archeologico Pietro Griffo, Agrigento
De Miro, 1984-1985, L'attivita della soprintendenza archeologica di Agrigento (Anni 1980-84)

two dedications to a caesar by the annii
Five lines of Greek letters, centred on the wide face of the chest and laid out between guide lines.

Deeply cut lunate letters of somewhat varying height. Kappa has tall vertical, lower tail extends to the line; Pi has vertical strokes of equal length, some overlap by horizontal; Mu has extended serifs off the upper strokes; epsilon and sigma are lunate, the cross-bar of the epsilon sometimes very lightly cut and detached; alpha and delta have extended serifs on the upper strokes; there is a single comma-shaped interpunct in line 1 after the first letter.
Libertini, 1929, Il regio museo archeologico di Siracusa
Single surviving line of Latin letters, with vacat before.
